project_overview.project_name: Strengthening Institutions for Refugee Administration Project (SIRA)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve organizational and institutional capacity for managing refugees and host communities in Pakistan
project_overview.total_financing: US$50 million (SDR 36.2 million equivalent) - comprising IDA Credit of US$29.16 million and IDA Grant of US$20.84 million
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 1.4 million Afghan refugees in Pakistan and host communities. Direct beneficiaries include approximately 15,000 PoR card holders (25% female) estimated to benefit from visa policy. Secondary beneficiaries include CCAR and CAR staff and other government agencies involved in refugee management

primary_locations: Four provincial Commissionerates for Afghan Refugees (CARs)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Project operates in refugee-affected and hosting areas (RAHA) across Pakistan. While Pakistan is not classified as fragile, the project addresses protracted refugee situation and operates in areas with refugee concentrations. KP and Balochistan have significant refugee populations and security concerns

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Implementing Organizational and Institutional Reforms for Improved Management of Refugees and Host Communities (Performance-based)
key_components.description: Performance-based financing component supporting key reforms under Government's Repatriation and Management Policy (RMP). Finances eligible expenditure programs (EEPs) including employee-related expenses, operating expenses, and repairs/maintenance for SAFRON, Ministry of Interior, and Pakistan Bureau of Statistics. Linked to seven Disbursement-Linked Indicators (DLIs) measuring: complaints handling mechanisms, management information systems, visa issuance performance, organizational capacity, staff knowledge/skills, socio-economic data collection, and visa policy implementation

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Technical Assistance for Improved Management of Refugees and Host Communities
key_components.description: Supports specific inputs for institutional and organizational capacity strengthening. Six sub-components: (2.1) Strengthening CCAR/CARs institutional capacity through skills audits, training, organizational reviews, MIS development, legal framework review, and Refugee Learning Program; (2.2) Improving RMP implementation including visa policy design, information sharing systems, and complaints handling mechanisms; (2.3) Government stakeholder engagement on refugee initiatives and data protection; (2.4) Supporting CARs in strategic communication and host community engagement; (2.5) Establishing baseline and regular data collection on selected indicators; (2.6) CCAR project implementation and management support including safeguards, M&E, and audits

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of States and Frontier Regions (SAFRON), with Chief Commissionerate for Afghan Refugees (CCAR) managing day-to-day implementation
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Inter-Ministerial Steering Committee (IMSC) chaired by Secretary SAFRON, meeting semi-annually to provide strategic direction. Interprovincial Working Group (IPWG) chaired by Chief Commissioner meeting quarterly. Operations Support Unit (OSU) led by Project Coordinator reporting to Chief Commissioner. Coordination with Ministry of Interior (MoI), Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MoFA), Ministry of Human Rights (MoHR), NADRA, and Pakistan Bureau of Statistics
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for Borrowers. Dedicated Procurement Committee established as review body. Procurement specialist hired as part of OSU. Bank-conducted training on procurement and contract management. Procurement profile includes IT equipment, office equipment/supplies, and vehicles through national Request for Bids. High procurement risk mitigated through capacity building and close oversight
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Dedicated M&E unit in CCAR responsible for data collection and project performance monitoring. Third-party verification agent validates DLI achievement and submits verification reports. 13 results indicators: 5 PDO-level indicators, 7 DLIs, and intermediate results indicators. Quarterly progress reports required within 15 days of reporting cycle end. Annual audits by Auditor General of Pakistan (AGP). Interim Unaudited Financial Reports (IUFRs) submitted semi-annually

identified_risks.risk_description: Project supports administrative and policy actions that could encounter resistance. Reputational risk of perceptions that World Bank is supporting repatriation of Afghan refugees. Political nature of refugee issues in Pakistan creates risk of reversal if political configurations change. Pakistan's political and governance situation remains stable but refugee issues are politically sensitive
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project supports only elements of existing Government policy approved in February 2017 and administrative actions linked to strengthening CCAR institutional capacity. Bank will monitor activities related to passport issuance in Afghanistan under EZ-KAR Project. Provincial Bank-supported projects will implement strong strategic communication program

identified_risks.risk_description: Pakistan undergoing economic stabilization supported by IMF program. Fiscal impact could lead to delayed release of capital and recurrent budgets for Project implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Bank will work with GoP to prioritize expenditures. Continued coordination with donor agencies active in humanitarian sector to absorb potential economic impact on Project

identified_risks.risk_description: Refugee policy involves multiple entities making consensus difficult. Negative perceptions and security concerns about continued refugee presence could influence stakeholder engagement
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Inter-ministerial committee already formed to support interagency coordination. Strategic communication program targeting specific stakeholders with key messages

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design developed jointly by SAFRON and World Bank based on Government priorities. Design incorporates lessons from World Bank experience in governance and public sector management

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Participating agencies have low capacity. Specific problems include retention of qualified staff and procurement of goods/services where capacity gaps and procedural aspects cause delays. CCAR and CARs require enhanced capacity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Enhanced Bank support to build capacity of relevant agencies. Strong communications strategy around reform. Dedicated procurement and financial management specialists in OSU. Bank-conducted training sessions

identified_risks.risk_description: Systemic weaknesses in procurement capacity. SAFRON implementing Bank project for first time. Limited procurement capacity in implementing agency
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Additional capacity for procurement and contract management provided. Close oversight of procurement processes and contracts. Dedicated Procurement Committee. Bank training on procurement and contract management. Financial management subject to audit by AGP and regular scrutiny of IUFRs. Regular Bank support

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risk rating. Project does not finance construction or require land acquisition/resettlement
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design informed by technical assessments addressing social issues. Project communications provide stakeholder consultation opportunities. Complaints handling and beneficiary feedback mechanisms ensure prompt complaint resolution. Social development specialist in OSU coordinates social and gender aspects

identified_risks.risk_description: Negative perceptions and security concerns around continued refugee presence could influence stakeholder engagement. Multiple stakeholders with varying interests
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project communication strategy targets specific stakeholders with key messages. IMSC supports inter-agency coordination. Community-led meetings for consultation on visa policy implementation

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk that elements of Pakistan's Protection Framework could be changed given evolving regional situation, impacting framework objectives. Possibility of new data protection legislation affecting how refugee personal data is collected, stored, and used
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ensure Protection Framework remains adequate as confirmed by UNHCR. Work with Government to strengthen data protection regime. Project will work closely with UNHCR to ensure strict adherence to existing protocols agreed between NADRA and UNHCR. Design data integrity protocol for periodic reporting on data breaches

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project will develop and incorporate citizen engagement elements throughout implementation. Robust communication plan and complaints handling mechanisms established. Project-supported activities include mechanism for consulting affected communities through community-led meetings. Communities and individuals can submit complaints to project-level grievance redress mechanisms or World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS). World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints regarding Bank non-compliance with policies
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project aims to close gender gaps in visa policy and staffing. Visa Facilitation Centers (VFCs) will have dedicated lines for women applicants and female staff trained to ensure equal access. Project communication strategy includes specific messages targeting female refugees to educate on opportunities and address cultural biases. CCAR and CARs will increase share of women employees from less than 10% to 15-20%. Human resource policies revised to encourage women employment. OSU will dedicate preferably at least 50% of positions to female employees with training opportunities. Project indicators track: share of women employees in CCAR/CAR, share of women receiving visas, share benefiting from CCAR training. Gender impact assessment of visa policy to be conducted before Midterm Review
vulnerable_groups: Afghan refugees (1.4 million, nearly half women)
Refugee women and girls facing particular difficulties due to cultural barriers restricting access to social services
Refugees in urban areas (68% of Afghan refugees live outside refugee villages)
Female refugees facing constraints in accessing education, health services, employment due to constrained mobility, lack of information, social segregation, and lack of financial means

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project addresses protracted refugee situation resulting from Afghanistan conflict. Supports institutional capacity for managing refugees and host communities affected by conflict-driven displacement. Project design informed by development approaches to forced displacement. Supports voluntary repatriation, documentation of undocumented Afghans, visa policy implementation, and border management as per Government's Repatriation and Management Policy (RMP). Complements Afghanistan EZ-KAR project supporting passport issuance to Afghans in Pakistan. Project supports Solutions Strategy for Afghan Refugees (SSAR) with three pillars: support voluntary repatriation, promote sustainable reintegration in Afghanistan, provide continued assistance to host communities
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Pakistan hosts 1.4 million Afghan refugees (down from 5 million at peak), representing protracted refugee situation since 1970s. Refugees initially lived in special villages but since mid-1990s relatively free to move to urban areas. 68% of Afghan refugees now live in urban areas outside refugee villages. Refugee situation creates pressure on public services in host communities. Government has invested in refugee protection framework consistent with international standards. Key fragility factors: institutional gaps in refugee management, organizational capacity gaps in CCAR/CARs, skills gaps in refugee protection/migration policy/MIS/procurement/financial management, diversity gap with women underrepresented (less than 10% of CCAR staff), lack of systems for host community engagement, absence of clear urban refugee management policy, security concerns about refugee presence, political sensitivity of refugee issues
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project operates in context of security concerns about continued refugee presence in Pakistan. Negative perceptions and security concerns could influence stakeholder engagement. Project supports administrative actions for refugee management rather than direct humanitarian assistance. Visa policy implementation includes outreach sessions in top ten refugee-hosting districts. Project supports border management improvements at Torkham to regulate cross-border movements. Data protection and personal data security emphasized given sensitivity of refugee information. Project maintains MoU on Registration of Afghan Citizens and UNHCR-NADRA Partnership Agreement incorporating UNHCR's Policy on Personal Data Protection
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Project directly addresses refugee situation. Pakistan hosts 1.4 million Afghan refugees (nearly half women). Project supports: (1) Voluntary repatriation facilitation (6,000+ repatriated in 2019); (2) Documentation of undocumented Afghans (879,000 documented in 2018, estimated 500,000 still undocumented); (3) Visa policy implementation for registered Afghan refugees; (4) Regulated border management; (5) Support to host communities affected by refugee presence. Project targets approximately 15,000 PoR card holders (25% female) to benefit from visa policy during project life. Supports both refugees and host communities through improved institutional capacity, complaints handling mechanisms, data collection on socio-economic characteristics, and livelihood opportunities
